summary: Monster Franklin's Tower, strong Jack Straw, high‑energy second set
blurb: The show builds from a loose first set into a powerhouse second set highlighted by a soaring 17‑minute Franklin’s Tower and an inspired Jack Straw. Jerry’s vocals swing between wounded and fiery while Keith steadies the keys after early hiccups. Overall it’s a solid ’78 performance despite some off‑beat moments.
review:
April 10, 1978 at Atlanta’s Fox Theater captures the Grateful Dead in classic ‘78 form – a first set that stumbles a bit but gives way to a second‑set eruption. After a tentative start (Bob’s late entry on “The Music Never Stopped” and drummers sounding “a bit drunk”), the band locks in for an unforgettable 17‑minute “Franklin's Tower,” described by several listeners as a “monster” jam, and follows it with a blistering “Jack Straw.” Jerry Garcia alternates between wounded, out‑of‑sync vocal moments early on and wild, power‑chord‑driven guitar later, while Keith Godchaux finds his footing after an initially ahead‑of‑the‑beat start. Bob Weir’s vocals take center stage on “Passenger” and he peppers the set with his trademark jokes, some of which divide the audience. The night also shines on “Candyman,” “All Over Now,” “Brown Eyed Women,” “Cassidy,” “Peggy‑O,” and “Black Peter.” Though the first half feels uneven, the overall energy, improvisational chaos, and a few standout numbers make this show a must‑listen for ’78 Deadheads.

For listeners seeking the best version, the Miller soundboard tape (gd1978-04-10.sbd.miller.108665.flac16) delivers clear, solid audio that captures the soaring towers and the rescuing Jack Straw without distracting flaws. Whether you’re after the raw power of “Franklin’s Tower” or the playful banter between songs, this recording offers the most balanced listening experience.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Multiple recordings praise the show’s high‑energy second set, monster Franklin's Tower and standout Jack Straw (+1.4). Mixed notes about early sloppiness and off‑beat playing temper the positivity but do not outweigh the overall enthusiasm.

best_recording.reason: Good soundboard source with clear mix; captures the monster Franklin's Tower and strong Jack Straw without technical flaws, offering the most balanced listening experience.

key_highlights: Monster 17‑minute Franklin’s Tower that defines the second set
Outstanding Jack Straw performance rescuing a sloppy first half
High‑energy improvisation on Cassidy, Peggy‑O and Black Peter
Solid soundboard audio despite early tightness in rhythm section

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia swings between wounded, out‑of‑sync vocals early and fiery, power‑chord guitar later, delivering hot licks throughout the night.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh provides a solid bass foundation with no notable issues mentioned; his playing underpins the band's improvisational flights.
band_performance.Bob: Weir’s vocal timing drifts at times and his jokes split opinions, but he shines on “Passenger” and drives the energy forward.
band_performance.Mickey: Drums start a bit loose—described as ‘drunk’ early—but settle into a playful groove that fuels the second‑set jams.
band_performance.Billy: The dual drumming is uneven at first, with long drum sections feeling off‑beat, yet later locks in to support the soaring improvisations.
